result:
At dawn, the city seems to inhale before anyone has remembered to be hurried. Delivery trucks idle beside shuttered cafes, pigeons confer on traffic lights, and a newspaper lies folded like a secret on a wet stoop. I like that hour because it belongs to no single purpose. The baker is measuring flour, the nurse is walking home under a lavender sky, the programmer is still awake, convinced one more line will solve everything. For a few minutes their lives overlap without colliding.

I think attention is a kind of hospitality. When we really notice a thing, a cracked tile, a stranger's careful braid, the blue reflection in a puddle, we offer it room inside us. Nothing grand has to happen. The ordinary world, repeatedly welcomed, becomes inexhaustible. A spoon ringing against a mug can be a bell. Steam from oatmeal can be weather. Even waiting at a crosswalk can turn ceremonial if you feel the crowd leaning forward together, trusting the little white figure to appear.

Later the day hardens into schedules, receipts, messages, decisions. Still, some of the morning's spaciousness can be carried along. It hides in pauses before answering, in choosing the slower route, in looking up from a screen when sirens pass. Maybe freedom is not always escape. Maybe it is the practiced ability to find a window, even in a wall, and let the light through. Each morning, if we listen, teaches the same quiet lesson: begin again, gently, with open eyes and hands unclenched.

At dawn, the city seems to inhale before anyone has remembered to be hurried. Delivery trucks idle beside shuttered cafes, pigeons confer on traffic lights, and a newspaper lies folded like a secret on a wet stoop. I like that hour because it belongs to no single purpose. The baker is measuring flour, the nurse is walking home under a lavender sky, the programmer is still awake, convinced one more line will solve everything. For a few minutes their lives overlap without colliding.

I think attention is a kind of hospitality. When we really notice a thing, a cracked tile, a stranger's careful braid, the blue reflection in a puddle, we offer it room inside us. Nothing grand has to happen. The ordinary world, repeatedly welcomed, becomes inexhaustible. A spoon ringing against a mug can be a bell. Steam from oatmeal can be weather. Even waiting at a crosswalk can turn ceremonial if you feel the crowd leaning forward together, trusting the little white figure to appear.

Later the day hardens into schedules, receipts, messages, decisions. Still, some of the morning's spaciousness can be carried along. It hides in pauses before answering, in choosing the slower route, in looking up from a screen when sirens pass. Maybe freedom is not always escape. Maybe it is the practiced ability to find a window, even in a wall, and let the light through. Each morning, if we listen, teaches the same quiet lesson: begin again, gently, with open eyes and hands unclenched.
